classification: Open-source web application fuzzer (HTTP request fuzzer)
short_description: Wfuzz is a Python-based web fuzzer that injects payloads into HTTP requests to discover endpoints, parameters, and behaviors, and to triage responses using flexible filters and output formats.
overview_notes: Wfuzz is a general-purpose web application fuzzer that operates by replacing FUZZ tokens embedded in any part of an HTTP request (URL path/query, headers, cookies, body, or method) with values from one or more payload generators. It is widely used in web and API security assessments for endpoint discovery, parameter enumeration, and behavior analysis under varying inputs. The project has long-running maintenance history and active releases on GitHub, with documentation hosted on Read the Docs. It is not a full vulnerability scanner; rather, it is a request generator and response triage framework that relies on user-chosen wordlists and filters.
what_it_is_notes:
- A command-line and library tool that automates HTTP fuzzing using a simple token-replacement model (FUZZ/FUZnZ) and a rich plugin system for payloads, encoders/decoders, printers, and filters. 
- Supports fuzzing across request components (URL, query parameters, form/JSON bodies, headers, cookies, and HTTP verbs) and can operate through proxies and with HTTP authentication. 
- Provides a filter language to match or exclude responses by status codes, size/words/lines, regexes, headers, and other metadata. 
- Outputs can be rendered or exported via printers (e.g., JSON/CSV/HTML) and consumed programmatically via a Python library API.
how_it_works_notes:
- Core mechanism: wherever the FUZZ (or additional position markers) appear in the request template, Wfuzz iterates payload values and sends corresponding HTTP requests. Multiple payloads can be combined for multi-position fuzzing. 
- Payload system: built-in generators include files/wordlists, lists, ranges, and more; encoders/decoders (e.g., URL, base64, hashing) can transform payloads before injection. 
- Response triage: a filter expression language enables pre/post filtering and selection by HTTP response attributes (status, length, words/lines, regex matches, headers). 
- Execution controls: supports concurrency tuning and delays, recursion for discovered paths, proxy chaining (HTTP/SOCKS), and Basic/Digest/NTLM authentication. 
- Library mode: a Python API mirrors the CLI concepts for integration into custom tooling and pipelines.
inputs: HTTP request template containing one or more FUZZ tokens in the URL, method, headers, cookies, or body
Payload sources (e.g., wordlists, lists, ranges) and optional encoders/decoders
Filter/matcher expressions (status codes, size/words/lines, regex, headers)
outputs: Enumerated requests and corresponding response metadata (status, size, words/lines)
Filtered result sets highlighting potentially interesting responses
Structured exports via printers (e.g., JSON, CSV, HTML) for downstream analysis
Programmatic result objects when used via the Python library
core_concepts: Payload plugins and iterators (files, lists, ranges)
Filter language for triage and pre/post filtering
Printers for output formatting and exports
typical_workflow: Define a target request and injection points by placing FUZZ markers in the appropriate request components.
Select payloads/wordlists and any encoders/decoders needed to match API formats (e.g., URL-encoding, base64).
Configure filters to reduce noise (e.g., hide common 404s, focus on specific statuses or length deltas).
Tune execution (threads, delays, proxies, auth) to respect target stability and rate limits.
Run fuzzing, review triaged results, and refine payloads/filters iteratively.
Optionally recurse into discovered paths or export structured results for further analysis in other tools.
use_cases: API endpoint and resource discovery by fuzzing paths and versions across gateways or microservices
Parameter discovery and behavior analysis for REST/JSON APIs, including hidden or undocumented inputs
Validation of input handling and content negotiation by fuzzing headers (e.g., Content-Type, Accept) and cookies
Authentication and authorization surface probing (e.g., header/cookie permutations, verb tampering) within permitted tests
Baseline and differential response analysis to flag anomalies that merit manual verification
Evaluating rate limiting and throttling behavior under controlled concurrency and pacing
Testing for HTTP parameter pollution indicators or inconsistent parameter parsing across components
WAF/gateway behavior exploration via header variations and encodings (within authorized scopes)
limitations: Not a vulnerability scanner; it does not confirm exploitability or risk without analyst interpretation.
Effectiveness depends on payload quality and filters; poor wordlists or triage can yield noise or false negatives.
May trigger rate limiting, WAFs, or account lockouts; requires careful pacing and authorization.
Lacks stateful, model-based, or coverage-guided fuzzing typical of specialized API fuzzers; complex workflows (chained requests, CSRF/anti-automation) require external orchestration.
Performance is adequate but generally slower than some compiled fuzzers; heavy runs can be resource-intensive.
Documentation versions lag behind the newest releases; some features/flags may differ across 2.x vs 3.x lines.

related_tools.name: ffuf
related_tools.relationship: Go-based fast web fuzzer with similar token model; often chosen for speed and directory/parameter discovery.

related_tools.name: Burp Suite (Intruder/Turbo Intruder)
related_tools.relationship: Commercial proxy suite with request fuzzing and high-throughput attack modules; complementary for interactive testing and complex session handling.

related_tools.name: OWASP ZAP (Fuzzer)
related_tools.relationship: Open-source proxy with built-in fuzzer; overlaps in request fuzzing and triage features.

related_tools.name: Arjun
related_tools.relationship: Parameter discovery tool; complements Wfuzz for finding hidden GET/POST/JSON parameters.

related_tools.name: Param Miner (Burp extension)
related_tools.relationship: Automates parameter discovery/mining; complementary to payload-driven fuzzing.

related_tools.name: Gobuster/Dirb/Dirbuster
related_tools.relationship: Focused directory and DNS brute-forcers; overlap on endpoint enumeration but narrower scope.

related_tools.name: Schemathesis
related_tools.relationship: Property-based testing of APIs from OpenAPI/Swagger; more model-aware than generic fuzzers like Wfuzz.

related_tools.name: RESTler
related_tools.relationship: Stateful, specification-based API fuzzer; contrasts with Wfuzz’s black-box token-replacement approach.

evidence_gaps: Official documentation on Read the Docs primarily covers 2.1.x/2.4-era features, while GitHub shows 3.0.x releases; a consolidated 3.x feature reference is limited.

Direct, official statements about API-specific capabilities (e.g., JSON schema-aware fuzzing or multi-step state handling) are not present; usage for APIs is inferred from general HTTP fuzzing capability.
Public performance benchmarks versus other fuzzers (e.g., ffuf) are scarce in authoritative sources.
